critic_raw:
This is a carefully designed experiment with genuine strengths: temporal splitting, dual-label strategy, stratified reporting, and explicit confound controls. However, several methodological issues undermine the core claim — that the neural model is being fairly compared to BM25, specifically on long-tail clinical queries.

**Issue 1: The fine-tuned neural model is evaluated on a test distribution it implicitly shaped through the training pipeline, while BM25 is not.**

The methodology fine-tunes the dual-encoder on training triplets derived from the same query log that generated the implicit labels. If the hospital's BM25 (or any lexical system) was the production retrieval system during months 1–24, then the click labels are conditioned on what BM25 chose to show users — a well-documented feedback loop. The neural model is trained to predict relevance given BM25-surfaced documents, so on common queries (where BM25 surfaces good results and clicks are reliable), it learns to mimic BM25. On rare queries, BM25 surfaces poor results, clicks are sparse or noisy, and the neural model gets weak training signal. The hypothesis predicts the neural model wins on rare queries, but the mechanism of the experiment makes this the hardest regime for the neural model to learn in. A genuine win on tail queries would be remarkable evidence *despite* the training signal bias, but the design does not acknowledge that the training labels systematically under-represent relevance for long-tail queries relative to the true relevance distribution. Evidence one way or the other: compare the density of training triplets per query frequency quartile; if rare queries have 10x fewer positive pairs than common queries, the training signal argument is confirmed. A blind human relevance assessment of neural model outputs on tail queries (not using the click-derived labels at all) would also be diagnostic.

**Issue 2: Retraining on training + validation combined after hyperparameter selection with nDCG@10 creates asymmetric tuning advantage for the neural model.**

BM25's k1 and b are tuned on the validation set using nDCG@10. The neural model's hyperparameters are also tuned on the validation set, then the neural model is retrained on 30 months of data. But BM25 parameters k1 and b are not sensitive to training set size in the same way — they are corpus statistics-independent tuning constants. The neural model, by contrast, sees 25% more training data in its final form than the model that was hyperparameter-selected. There is no equivalent expansion for BM25. This is not a tuning effort symmetry problem (the design claims equivalent tuning effort); it is a training data asymmetry: the final neural model is a strictly different model than the validated one, while the final BM25 is identical to the validated one. If the extra 6 months of data contain query patterns more similar to the test distribution (months 31–36) due to temporal proximity, the neural model benefits disproportionately. Evidence: report validation-set nDCG for the model trained on 24 months vs. the model trained on 30 months. If they diverge meaningfully, the comparison is confounded.

**Issue 3: The 10-second dwell time threshold for defining relevance is unjustified and asymmetrically harmful to long-tail query labeling.**

The design discards clicks with <10 seconds dwell time as 'accidental.' In clinical settings, query intent varies sharply: an experienced clinician retrieving a familiar drug-interaction table may confirm relevance in 3 seconds; a trainee reading an unfamiliar protocol may dwell for 90 seconds without the document being relevant. The 10-second threshold treats dwell time as a proxy for relevance uniformly across user roles and document types, but the methodology explicitly stratifies user roles for confound checks — acknowledging that role matters — without adjusting the labeling rule accordingly. More critically for the hypothesis: long-tail queries are disproportionately asked by less-experienced users (who may not know exactly what they need), and rare clinical documents are longer and more complex, inflating dwell time for irrelevant results. This means the implicit labels on tail queries may be *more* polluted by false positives (long dwell on a wrong but confusing document) than labels on common queries, inverting the expected noise direction. The manual annotation of 300 tail queries partially mitigates this, but those 300 queries are drawn from the same implicit-label pool for selection, not independently sampled from the full query space. Evidence: compare mean dwell time distributions stratified by query frequency quartile and user role; if rare queries show higher dwell time variance, the threshold's effect is asymmetric and the tail-query training signal is more corrupted than the design assumes.

**Issue 4: The prerequisite assumption — that BERT-base encoders trained with InfoNCE on implicit clinical feedback will produce semantically meaningful embeddings for rare clinical terminology — is unverified.**

The hypothesis is that a neural semantic retriever outperforms BM25 on long-tail queries *because* it captures semantic relationships beyond lexical overlap. This mechanism requires that the encoders have learned meaningful representations for rare clinical terms. BERT-base is pretrained on general text; its subword tokenizer handles rare clinical acronyms by decomposing them into subwords that may have no semantic relationship to the clinical concept (e.g., 'MRSA' → 'MR', '##SA'). Fine-tuning with InfoNCE on implicit labels will not fix this if the rare terms appear too infrequently in training triplets to update the embeddings significantly (see Issue 1 on sparse training signal for tail queries). If the encoders are not meaningfully representing rare clinical terms, the neural model's apparent gains on tail queries could be driven by surface-level correlations (document length, section structure, common co-occurring terms) rather than the claimed semantic matching. The design does not include any embedding quality check for rare clinical terms. Evidence: compute nearest-neighbor purity for rare clinical acronyms in embedding space before and after fine-tuning; if rare acronyms cluster with semantically unrelated terms, the mechanism is broken. Alternatively, ablate by replacing the fine-tuned encoder with a frozen ClinicalBERT — if performance is similar, fine-tuning on this implicit signal added little.

**Issue 5: IDF weights frozen from the full 36-month corpus introduce future information into BM25 scoring for documents in the training period.**

The design states: 'IDF weights are computed once from the indexed corpus and then frozen for all training, validation, and test-time scoring.' If the indexed corpus includes documents from months 25–36 (the validation and test periods), then IDF weights for terms that appear primarily in newer documents will be deflated by future document additions. BM25 scores on training-period documents will use IDF values that reflect a corpus state that did not exist at training time. This is a subtle leakage: the relative rareness of clinical terms shifts over time (new procedures get documented, old ones deprecated), and using future-aware IDF values changes which documents are scored as highly relevant during training. For the neural model, the contrastive training loss does not directly use IDF, so this leakage affects the label quality (which documents appear as positive pairs) rather than the scoring function. The practical magnitude is probably small for stable clinical terminology but could be non-trivial for rapidly evolving clinical vocabulary (COVID protocols, new drug approvals). Evidence: recompute BM25 scores using IDF frozen at month 24 vs. month 36; if score rankings change materially for any query stratum, the leakage is real.
all_issues_raised: Training labels are conditioned on the production retrieval system's output (likely BM25), creating a feedback loop that systematically under-represents relevance signal for long-tail queries — the exact stratum the hypothesis targets.

The neural model is retrained on 30 months of data after hyperparameter selection on 24 months, while the final BM25 is identical to its validated form — this is a training data asymmetry, not tuning effort symmetry.

The 10-second dwell time threshold for relevance is applied uniformly across user roles and document types, but long-tail queries are disproportionately associated with longer dwell times for irrelevant documents, inflating false positive rates in the tail-query training signal.
The prerequisite assumption that BERT-base encoders will learn meaningful representations for rare clinical terminology after fine-tuning on sparse implicit labels is unverified and may not hold.

IDF weights frozen from the full 36-month corpus encode future document additions into BM25 scores during training, constituting a mild temporal leakage for any clinical terms whose corpus frequency changes significantly over the study period.

The prerequisite assumption that BERT-base encoders will learn meaningful representations for rare clinical terminology after fine-tuning on sparse implicit labels is unverified and may not hold.
